note_data.title: Forces and motion
keyPoints: **Force** is a push or pull that can change the shape, size, or motion of an object
**Mass** is the amount of matter in an object and is measured in kilograms (kg)
**Weight** is the gravitational force acting on an object and is measured in newtons (N)
Weight = mass × gravitational field strength (W = mg)
**Scalar quantities** have magnitude only (mass, speed, distance, time)
**Vector quantities** have both magnitude and direction (force, weight, velocity, acceleration, momentum)
In free fall, all objects accelerate at approximately 10 m/s² due to gravity (ignoring air resistance)
**Momentum** = mass × velocity (p = mv) and is conserved in collisions
Terminal velocity is reached when air resistance equals weight, resulting in zero acceleration

definitions.definition: A push or pull that can change the shape, size, or motion of an object
The amount of matter in an object
The gravitational force acting on an object
The product of mass and velocity of a moving object
The constant velocity reached when the drag force equals the driving force

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Show the formula, substitute values, and give the final answer with correct units
Give clear differences between two concepts using 'whereas' or 'but'

modelAnswer.question: A cricket ball of mass 0.16 kg is dropped from the roof of a building in Lahore. Calculate the weight of the ball. (gravitational field strength = 10 N/kg)
modelAnswer.answer:
Weight = mass × gravitational field strength
W = mg
W = 0.16 × 10
W = 1.6 N
markBreakdown: Mark 3: Correct answer with units (1.6 N)

commonMistakes: Never say mass and weight are the same — mass is measured in kg, weight in N
Students often forget that weight changes with location but mass stays constant
Never say momentum is a scalar — it is a vector quantity with direction
Never forget units in calculations — weight must be in newtons (N)
examinerTips: Forces and motion appears in every Paper 2 — especially weight calculations
Always show the formula first, then substitute values for calculation questions
Remember to state direction when dealing with vector quantities
Free fall questions often test understanding that acceleration = 10 m/s² regardless of mass
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Free body diagram showing forces acting on a falling object: weight (downward arrow) and air resistance (upward arrow), with arrows drawn to scale to show relative magnitudes
